ocr.text: The following data table presents a detailed analysis of the relationship between perceived helplessness and its associated
variables, as measured by the Child Post-Traumatic Cognitions Inventory - Short Version (CPTCI-SW). A significant finding
highlighted in the table is the item reflecting the belief, “I can’t stop bad things from happening to me,” with 50.2% of
respondents indicating agreement to varying extents.
Item  Agree a bit/agree a lot (%)  Subscale
I can’t stop bad things from happening to me  50.2  CPTCI-SW
Anyone could hurt me  41.1  CPTCI-SW
I’m scared that I’ll get so angry that I’ll break something or hurt someone  36.8  CPTCI-PC
I can’t cope when things get tough  35.9  CPTCI-SW
I have to watch out for danger all the time  32.0  CPTCI-SW
Variable  Odds ratio  95% Confidence interval  p-value
N past-malaria infections  1.18  0.39 – 3.55  0.772
Other occupations: gold miner  0.74  0.14 – 3.75  0.711
This metric underscores the prevalence of negative cognitive patterns that may contribute to a sense of helplessness among
individuals, particularly in contexts related to trauma or adverse experiences. Additionally, the odds ratio of 3.27 suggests a
strong association between this belief and the assessed subscale, indicating that those who endorse this thought are over three
times more likely to experience related adverse outcomes. The confidence interval ranging from 1.28 to 8.37 further illustrates
the variability and reliability of this association, while the p-value of 0.014 indicates statistical significance. These findings are
critical for understanding the psychological implications of perceived helplessness and may inform interventions aimed at
addressing cognitive distortions in affected populations.
